1: haytah alai yad-YHWH vayotzieni veruach YHWH ('the hand of the LORD was on me; the Spirit of the LORD carried me out'): the inaugural vision formula (cf. Ezekiel 1:1; Ezekiel 3:14,22; Ezekiel 8:1) — the characteristic language of Ezekiel's visionary transport. ruach YHWH (H7307, Spirit of the LORD) — the Spirit appears 9 times in Ezekiel 37:1-14, bracketing the entire vision. vayenicheni betouch habiq'a ('and set me down in the middle of the valley'): the same biq'ah (H1237, valley/plain) as Ezekiel 3:22 — the Chebar plain. The vision is located in the same space as Ezekiel's original commissioning vision.
2: vehi melea atzamot ('it was full of bones'): the valley of dry bones — Israel as the dead scattered across a battlefield. vaavir oti aleichem saviv saviv ('he led me around them on all sides'): the prophetic tour of the battlefield — a complete survey of the extent of the death before the resurrection is proclaimed. The careful observation of the death prepares the prophet and the reader for the magnitude of what follows.
3: vayomer elai ben-adam hatichyena haatzamot haeleh ('he said to me: son of man, can these bones live?'): the central question of the vision — posed as a genuine question to the prophet, not as a rhetorical certainty. vaomar adonai YHWH atah yadatah ('I said: Lord GOD, you know'): Ezekiel's response is the perfectly calibrated prophetic humility — he does not claim to know what is outside his sight. The answer to the question 'can these bones live?' is known only by YHWH. The prophetic posture of epistemic dependence is the right posture before the divine possibility of resurrection.
4: vayomer elai hinabbe al-haatzamot haeleh ('he said to me: prophesy over these bones'): the command to prophesy to the dead — an act that demonstrates that prophetic speech participates in the divine creative act. The prophet speaks into non-existence and calls it into existence. shim'u devar YHWH ('hear the word of the LORD'): even the dead bones are commanded to hear — the word of YHWH has power that bypasses the apparent impossibility.
5: koh amar adonai YHWH laatzamot haeleh ('thus says the Lord GOD to these bones'): YHWH speaking directly to dead bones — the oracle addressed to the non-living is the most radical deployment of prophetic speech in the OT. hineh ani mebi bachem ruach vicheeytem ('I will cause breath to enter you and you will live'): ruach (H7307, breath/wind/spirit) — the same word as in Genesis 2:7 where YHWH breathes into Adam's nostrils. The valley of dry bones is a second creation, recapitulating the first.
6: venatatti aleichem gidin vehaaleti aleichem basar ('I will put sinews on you and bring flesh on you'): the anatomical sequence — sinews (H1517, gidin), flesh (H1320, basar), skin (H5785, or). The physiological reconstitution of a human being in precise order: the structural framework (bones) → sinews (tendons binding bones) → flesh (muscle) → skin (covering). The reversal of decomposition. venatatif lachem ruach vicheeytem: the breath/spirit given last — structure without spirit is still a corpse; the order matters.
7: venibbeti kaasher tzuviti vatashav ruach vetiqqerenah atzamot atzem el-atzmo ('as I prophesied there was a noise, and a rattling, and the bones came together, bone to bone'): the great noise (qol, sound) and the rattling (rash, H7494, earthquake/rattling/commotion) of bones assembling — the auditory experience of resurrection. atzem el-atzmo ('bone to its bone') — each bone finding its anatomical counterpart: precise, purposeful assembly. The chaos of the battlefield is reorganized into living bodies.
8: veraiti vehinneh aleichem gidin uvasar alah vatiqra aleichem or ('I looked, and sinews and flesh appeared on them and skin covered them over'): the visible sequence of reconstitution — the prophet watches it happen layer by layer. veruach ayin bahem ('but there was no breath/spirit in them'): the corpses are anatomically complete but not yet living — a crucial observation. Physical reconstitution alone does not constitute life; the Spirit must be separately given. The two-stage resurrection emphasizes the independence of the Spirit's gift.
9: vayomer elai hinabbe el-haruach ('he said to me: prophesy to the breath'): the second prophetic command — now to the ruach itself. koh amar adonai YHWH merarba ruchot boi haruach ('thus says the Lord GOD: come from the four winds, O breath'): me-arba ruchot ('from the four winds') — north, south, east, west — the breath/spirit gathered from the entire cosmos to fill the reconstituted bodies. The cosmic gathering of the Spirit parallels the cosmic gathering of the exiles in Ezekiel 37:21. ufchi baharugim haeleh viyichyu ('breathe into these slain that they may live'): the exact parallel to Genesis 2:7 (vayipach beapav nishmat chayyim).
10: vatavo bahem haruach vayyichyu vayyiqumu al-ragleichem ('the breath entered them and they lived and stood on their feet'): the sequence of resurrection: breath enters → they lived (chayyah, H2421) → stood on their feet. chayil gadol meod meod ('a very, very great army'): from dead bones scattered on the battlefield to a vast living army. The hyperbolic meod meod ('exceedingly very great') emphasizes the completeness of the reversal — not merely survival but abundant vitality.
11: ben-adam haatzamot haeleh kol-beit yisrael hemah ('son of man, these bones are the whole house of Israel'): the divine interpretation of the vision — the bones are not a future military event but present-tense Israel in exile. hinnam omrim yaveshu atzmoteinu veaveda tikvatenu ('they are saying: our bones are dried up and our hope is perished'): the national lament: tikvah (H8615, hope/cord/expectation) — the same word as in Job 17:15 (where can my hope be found?) and Jeremiah 29:11 (plans for a future and a hope). The exiles' despair is that hope itself has been destroyed — not merely delayed but eliminated.
12: lachen hinabbe veamarta aleichem koh amar adonai YHWH hineh ani potei'ach et-qivrotechem ('therefore prophesy and say to them: thus says the Lord GOD: I will open your graves'): the direct application of the vision to the national situation — YHWH will open graves and bring out (alah, H5927, cause to come up) the people from their graves. qever (H6913, grave/tomb) — exile as a kind of death-state. The return from exile is analogized to resurrection from the grave. vahaveti etchem el-admat yisrael: the land of Israel as the destination of the resurrection.
13: veyidattem ki-ani YHWH befotchi et-qivrotechem ('you will know that I am the LORD when I open your graves'): the recognition formula attached to the resurrection act — the knowledge of YHWH is the purpose of the resurrection. The resurrection event is epistemological: it teaches Israel who YHWH is in a way that nothing else could. uvehaaloti etchem miqivrotechem ami ('and when I bring you up from your graves, my people'): the covenant designation ami ('my people') — restored at the moment of resurrection. The resurrection constitutes the covenantal relationship.
14: venatatti ruchi bachem vicheeytem ('I will put my Spirit in you and you will live'): the synthesis of Ezekiel 36:27 and the valley vision — the Spirit given internally (Ezekiel 36:27) is the breath that animates the dry bones (Ezekiel 37:5-10). The two oracles are the same promise from different angles: the outward vision (dry bones to living army) and the inward promise (new heart, Spirit within). vehinnachti etchem al-admatechem ('I will put you down on your own land'): the final and ultimate settling — not temporary residence but permanent establishment (nuach, H5117, rest/settle permanently).
15: The new unit: two-sticks oracle (Ezekiel 37:15-28). The visual demonstration is different from the valley vision: instead of a landscape transformation, it is a hand-held enacted sign. Ezekiel holds two sticks, one for Judah and one for Ephraim/Joseph, and the two become one in his hand.
16: et le-Yehudah ('for Judah'): etz (H6086, tree/stick/wood) — a stick of writing material (a writing tablet or rod). ulveit Yisrael chaveirav ('for the house of Israel his companions') — the northern kingdom's associated tribes. veet-et Yosef asher beyad Efrayim ('the stick of Joseph which is in Ephraim's hand'): the dominant northern tribe gives its name to the Northern Kingdom. The two sticks represent the 931 BCE division of the united monarchy (1 Kgs 12) which had become permanent national separation.
17: veqarev otam echad el-echad lecha leetz echad vehayu leachad beyadechu ('join them together into one stick, and they will become one in your hand'): the enacted prophecy — the physical joining of two sticks in the prophet's hand demonstrates the political reunification of the divided kingdom. leetz echad ('to become one stick') — the oneness is specified: not merely cooperation but unity. The acted sign communicates what Israel's political history said was impossible.
18: The disciples' question: 'Will you not tell us what you mean by these?' — the acted prophecy requires interpretation, and the disciples recognize its interpretive demand. Ezekiel's sign-acts consistently create interpretive questions that provide the occasion for the divine explanation (cf. Ezekiel 12:9; Ezekiel 24:19). The question is part of the pedagogical design.
19: hineni loqei'ach et-etz Yosef asher beyad Efrayim ('I am taking the stick of Joseph which is in Ephraim's hand'): YHWH's own description of the sign-act, identifying with what the prophet performs. vesamti otam alav et-etz Yehudah vaasitim leetz echad ('and I will put them together — the stick of Judah — and they will become one stick'): the political reunification is explicitly YHWH's action. The political possibility of the sign is grounded in divine sovereign ability, not human political process.
20: The sticks held visibly in the prophet's hand — the public enactment of the oracle. The acted prophecy is communal: the sign is performed before the eyes of the exilic community, making the promise concrete and memorable. Even in Babylon, the exiles are watching a visual demonstration of their promised future.
21: velaqachti et-benei yisrael mibbein haggoyim asher halchu sham ('I will take the Israelites from among the nations where they have gone'): the gathering formula — laqach (take/gather) from among the nations. The second Exodus pattern: just as YHWH took Israel out of Egypt, he will take the scattered exiles from among the nations. veqibatzti otam missaviv ('I will gather them from all around'): the comprehensive gathering of diaspora from every direction.
22: vaasiti otam legoy echad baaretz beharei yisrael ulemelech echad yihyeh lahem lemelech lekulam ('I will make them one nation in the land on the mountains of Israel, and one king will be king over them all'): the political outcome of the restoration — goy echad (one nation) with melech echad (one king). The division of the Solomonic monarchy is permanently reversed. velo yihu od lishnei goyim ('they will no longer be two nations'): the permanent end of the north-south division. The two-kingdoms situation, which had persisted for four centuries, will not recur.
23: velo yitamme'u od begilluleihem ('they will no longer defile themselves with their idols'): the spiritual component of the restoration — freedom from idolatry. vehoshi'a otam miqqol moshevoteihem asher chatu bahem ('I will save them from all their apostasies'): yasha (H3467, save) — salvation from the specific sin of apostasy, not merely from external enemies. vetihartem vehayu li le'am ('I will cleanse them and they will be my people'): the covenant formula applied to the spiritually restored community.
24: veabdi David melech aleichem ('my servant David will be king over them'): the Davidic covenant renewed — the promised Davidic king who will shepherd the unified nation. David here is not the historical David (who died c.970 BCE) but the eschatological Davidic descendant, the anointed king promised in 2 Sam 7 and Ps 2; 89; 110. veroeh echad yihyeh lechulam ('one shepherd will be for all of them'): the shepherd-king of Ezekiel 34 is the Davidic descendant. uvechuqqotai yelekhu ('they will walk in my statutes'): the Spirit-enabled Torah-observance of 36:27 finds its political correlate in the Davidic ruler.
25: veyashvu al-haaretz asher natati leabdi leyaaqov asher yashvu vah avoteichem ('they will live in the land I gave to my servant Jacob — the land where your fathers lived'): the patriarchal land-grant renewed through Davidic-covenant language. The triple covenant connection: the land given to Jacob (patriarchal), the king from David (Davidic), and the Spirit-given Torah obedience (new covenant). venasi lahem le'olam ('a prince will be over them forever'): nasi le'olam — the eternal prince. The Davidic ruler's permanence answers the revolving-door kingship of Israel's historical kings.
26: vekarati lahem berit shalom ('I will make a covenant of peace with them'): berit shalom (H1285 + H7965, covenant of peace) — the fullest form of the new covenant promise (also Numbers 25:12; Isaiah 54:10). berit olam yihyeh otam ('it will be an eternal covenant with them'): berit olam — the permanent covenant that cannot be broken. The covenant of peace combines political security, agricultural abundance, and divine presence. untetim vehirbeitem venatatiti et-miqdashi betocham le'olam ('I will increase them and put my sanctuary in their midst forever'): the sanctuary — YHWH's permanent dwelling in the midst of the restored unified nation.
27: vehayah mishkani aleichem ('my dwelling will be over them'): mishkan (H4908, dwelling/tabernacle) — the tabernacle term, the mobile sanctuary of the wilderness period now made permanent. vehayiti lahem le'elohim vehem yihyu li le'am ('I will be their God and they will be my people'): the covenant formula for the last time in Ezekiel 37. The covenant formula appears at the end of the restoration section as the ultimate statement of what YHWH's restoration accomplishes: the covenant relationship fully reconstituted.
28: veyadu haggoyim ki ani YHWH meqaddesh et-yisrael ('the nations will know that I am the LORD who sanctifies Israel'): the recognition formula in the chapter's closing verse — but with a new epithet added: meqaddesh et-yisrael ('the one who sanctifies Israel'). YHWH's sanctifying of Israel is the means of his self-disclosure to the nations. The nations learn who YHWH is by watching what he does to Israel: restores, reunifies, gives a Davidic king, places his sanctuary, sanctifies. bihyot miqdashi betocham le'olam ('when my sanctuary is in their midst forever'): the sanctuary's permanent presence is the sign that the nations read.
